elements.sent-2411-001: We consider estimating the shared mean of a sequence of heavy-tailed random variables taking values in a Banach space.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, we revisit and extend a simple truncation-based mean estimator by Catoni and Giulini.
elements.sent-2411-003: While existing truncation-based approaches require a bound on the raw (non-central) second moment of observations, our results hold under a bound on either the central or non-central $p$th moment for some $p > 1$.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, our results hold for distributions with infinite variance.
elements.sent-2411-005: The main contributions of the paper follow from exploiting connections between truncation-based mean estimation and the concentration of martingales in 2-smooth Banach spaces.
elements.sent-2411-006: We prove two types of time-uniform bounds on the distance between the estimator and unknown mean: \bd{ inequalities , which can be optimized for a fixed sample size $n$, and non-asymptotic law of the iterated logarithm type inequalities, which match the tightness of line-crossing inequalities at all points in time up to a doubly logarithmic factor in $n$.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Our results do not depend on the dimension of the Banach space, hold under martingale dependence, and all constants in the inequalities are known and small.
elements.sent-2411-008: Mean estimation is perhaps the most important primitive in the statistician's toolkit.
elements.sent-2411-009: When the data is light-tailed (perhaps sub-Gaussian, sub-Exponential, or sub-Gamma), the sample mean is the natural estimator of this unknown population mean.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, when the data fails to have finite moments, the naive plug-in mean estimate is known to be sub-optimal.
elements.sent-2411-011: The failure of the plug-in mean has led to a rich literature focused on heavy-tailed mean estimation.
elements.sent-2411-012: In the univariate setting, statistics such as the thresholded/truncated mean estimator~\citep{tukey1963less, huber1981robust , trimmed mean estimator~\cite{oliveira2019sub, lugosi2021robust , median-of-means estimator~\citep{nemirovskij1983problem, jerrum1986random, alon1996space , and the Catoni M-estimator~\citep{catoni2017dimension, wang2023catoni have all been shown to exhibit favorable convergence guarantees.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: When a bound on the variance of the observations is known, many of these estimates enjoy sub-Gaussian rates of performance~\citep{lugosi2019mean , and this rate gracefully decays when only a bound on the $p$th central moment is known for some $p > 1$~\citep{bubeck2013bandits .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In the more challenging setting of multivariate heavy-tailed data, modern methods include the geometric median-of-means estimator~\citep{minsker2015geometric , the median-of-means tournament estimator~\citep{lugosi2019sub , and the truncated mean estimator~\citep{catoni2018dimension .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: We provide a more detailed account in Section~\ref{sec:related-work .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Of the aforementioned statistics, the truncated mean estimator is by far the simplest.
elements.sent-2411-017: This estimator, which involves truncating observations to lie within an appropriately-chosen ball centered at the origin, is extremely computationally efficient and can be updated online, very desirable for applied statistical tasks.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, this estimator also possesses a number of undesirable properties.
elements.sent-2411-019: First, it is not translation invariant, with bounds that depend on the raw moments of the random variables.
elements.sent-2411-020: Second, it requires a known bound on the $p$th moment of observations for some $p \geq 2$, thus requiring that the observations have finite variance.
elements.sent-2411-021: Third, bounds are only known in the setting of finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces --- convergence is not understood in the setting of infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces or Banach spaces.
elements.sent-2411-022: The question we consider here is simple: are the aforementioned deficiencies fundamental to truncation-based estimators, or can they be resolved with an improved analysis? The goal of this work is to show that the latter is true, demonstrating how a truncation-based estimator can be extended to handle fewer than two central moments in general classes of Banach spaces.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this work, we revisit and extend a simple truncation-based mean estimator due to \citet{catoni2018dimension .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Our estimator works by first using a small number of samples to produce a naive mean estimate, say through a sample mean.
elements.sent-2411-025: Then, the remaining sequence of observations is truncated to lie in an appropriately-sized ball centered at this initial mean estimate.
elements.sent-2411-026: These truncated samples are then averaged to provide a more robust estimate of a heavy-tailed mean.
elements.sent-2411-027: While existing works study truncation-based estimators via PAC-Bayesian analyses~\citep{catoni2018dimension, chugg2023time, langford2002pac , we find it more fruitful to study these estimators using tools from the theory of Banach space-valued martingales.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In particular, by proving a novel extension of classical results on the time-uniform concentration of bounded martingales due to \citet{pinelis1992approach, pinelis1994optimum , we are able to greatly improve the applicability of truncation-based estimators.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In particular, our estimator and analysis improves over that in \citep{catoni2018dimension in the following ways:.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The analysis holds in arbitrary 2-smooth Banach spaces instead of just finite-dimensional Euclidean space.
elements.sent-2411-031: This not only includes Hilbert spaces but also the commonly-studied $L^\alpha$ and $\ell^\alpha$ spaces for $2 \leq \alpha < \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our results require only a known upper bound on the conditional central $p$th moment of observations for some $p > 1$, and are therefore applicable to data lacking finite variance.
elements.sent-2411-033: Existing bounds for truncation estimators, on the other hand, require a bound on the non-central second moment.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our bounds are time-uniform and hold for data with a martingale dependence structure.
elements.sent-2411-035: We prove two types of inequalities: \bd{ inequalities , which can be optimized for a target sample size, and non-asymptotic law of the iterated logarithm (LIL) type inequalities, which match the tightness of the boundary-crossing inequalities at all times simultaneously up to a doubly logarithmic factor in the sample size.}
elements.sent-2411-036: We show that our estimator exhibits strong practical performance, and that our derived bounds are tighter than existing results in terms of constants.
elements.sent-2411-037: We run simulations which demonstrate that, for appropriate truncation diameters, the distance between our estimator and the unknown mean is tightly concentrated around zero.
elements.sent-2411-038: Informally, if we assume that the central $p$th moments of all observations are conditionally bounded by $v$, and we let $\wh{\mu _n$ denote our estimate after $n$ samples, then we show that \begin{equation}\|\wh{\mu}_n - \mu\| = O\left(v^{1/p}(\log(1/\delta)/n)^{\frac{p-1}{p}}\right) \qquad with probability  \geq 1 - \delta.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-039: As far as we are aware, the only other estimator to obtain the same guarantee in a similar setting is Minsker's geometric median-of-means~\citep{minsker2015geometric .}
elements.sent-2411-040: While we obtain the same rates, we feel that our truncation-style estimator has several benefits over geometric median-of-means.
elements.sent-2411-041: First, it is computationally lightweight and easy to compute exactly.
elements.sent-2411-042: Second, our \bd{ inequalities do not require as many tuning parameters to instantiate (eg choice of $\alpha^*$, $\gamma^*$, or $B$; see Section~\ref{sec:gmom-banach ).}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Third, we handle martingale dependence while Minsker does not.
elements.sent-2411-044: Finally, our analysis is significantly different from Minsker's---and from existing analyses of other estimators under heavy-tails---and may be of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-045: Section~\ref{sec:contributions discussed the relationship between this paper and the two most closely related works of \citet{catoni2018dimension and \citet{minsker2015geometric .}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: We now discuss how our work is related to the broader literature, none of which addresses our problem directly, but tackles simpler special cases of our problem (e.g., assuming more moments or boundedness, or with observations in Hilbert spaces or Euclidean spaces).
elements.paragraph-2411-001: Heavy-tailed mean estimation under independent observations.
elements.sent-2411-047: Truncation-based (also called threshold-based) estimators have a rich history in the robust statistics literature, dating back to works from Tukey, Huber, and others~\citep{huber1981robust,tukey1963less .}
elements.sent-2411-048: These estimators have either been applied in the univariate setting or in $\Re^d$ as in \citet{catoni2018dimension .}
elements.sent-2411-049: A related estimator is the so-called trimmed-mean estimator, which removes extreme observations and takes the empirical mean of the remaining points~\citep{oliveira2019sub,lugosi2021robust .}
elements.sent-2411-050: For real-valued observations with finite variance, the trimmed-mean has sub-Gaussian performance~\citep{oliveira2019sub .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Separately, \citet{catoni2017dimension introduce an approach for mean estimation in $\Re^d$ based on M-estimators with a family of appropriate influence functions.}
elements.sent-2411-052: This has come to be called ``Catoni's M-estimator.'' It requires at least two moments and fails to obtain sub-Gaussian rates.
elements.sent-2411-053: It faces the the additional burden of being less computationally efficient.
elements.sent-2411-054: A series of followup works have improved this estimator in various ways: \citet{chen2021generalized extend it to handle a $p$-th moment for $p\in(1,2)$ for real-valued observations, \citet{gupta2024beyond refine and sharpen the constants, and \citet{mathieu2022concentration studies the optimality of general M-estimators for mean estimation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Another important line of work on heavy-tailed mean estimation is based on median-of-means estimators~\citep{nemirovskij1983problem,jerrum1986random,alon1996space .}
elements.sent-2411-056: These estimators generally break a dataset into several folds, compute a mean estimate on each fold, and then compute some measure of central tendency amongst these estimates.
elements.sent-2411-057: For real-valued observations, \citet{bubeck2013bandits study a median-of-means estimator that holds under infinite variance.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Their estimator obtains the same rate as ours and Minsker's.
elements.sent-2411-059: Most relevant for our work is the result on geometric median-of-means due to \citet{minsker2015geometric , which can be used to aggregate several independent mean estimates in general separable Banach spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-060: In Hilbert spaces, when instantiated with the empirical mean under a finite variance assumption, geometric median-of-means is nearly sub-Gaussian (see discussion in Section~\ref{sec:contributions ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: We compare our threshold-based estimator extensively to geometric median-of-means in the sequel and demonstrate that we obtain the same rate of convergence.
elements.sent-2411-062: Another important result is the multivariate tournament median-of-means estimator due to ~\citet{lugosi2019sub .}
elements.sent-2411-063: For i.i.d.\ observations in $(\Re^d, \|\cdot\|_2)$ with shared covariance matrix (operator) $\Sigma$, then \citet{lugosi2019sub show this estimator can obtain the optimal sub-Gaussian rate of $O(\sqrt{\Tr(\Sigma)/n + \sqrt{\|\Sigma\|_{\text{op }\log(1/\delta)/n )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: However, this result requires the existence of a covariance matrix and does not extend to a bound on the $p$-th moment for $p \in (1, 2)$, which is the main focus of this work.
elements.sent-2411-065: While the original form of the tournament median-of-means estimator was computationally inefficient (with computation hypothesized to be NP-Hard in a survey by \citet{lugosi2019mean ), a computationally efficient approximation was developed by \citet{hopkins2020mean , with followup work improving the running time~\citep{cherapanamjeri2019fast .}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Tournament median-of-means was extended to general norms in $\Re^d$~\citep{lugosi2019near , though the authors note that this approach is still not computationally feasible.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Median-of-means style approaches have also been extended to general metric spaces~\citep{hsu2016loss,cholaquidis2024gros .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Of the above methods, only the geometric median-of-means estimator can handle observations that lack finite variance.
elements.sent-2411-069: Time-uniform concentration bounds, or concentration inequalities that are valid at data-dependent stopping times, have been the focus of significant recent attention~\citep{howard2020time,howard2021time, whitehouse2023time .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Such results are often obtained by identifying an underlying nonnegative supermartingale and then applying Ville's inequality~\citep{ville1939etude , a strategy that allows for martingale dependence quite naturally.}

elements.sent-2411-097: A space is separable if it contains a countable, dense subset, and a real-valued function $f:\B\to\Re$ is $(2,\beta)$-smooth if, for all $x,y\in\B$, $f(0) = 0$, $|f(x + y) - f(x)| \leq \|y\|$, and \begin{equation}\label{eq:2beta-smooth} f^2(x+y) + f^2(x - y) \leq 2f^2(x) + 2\beta^2 \|y\|^2.\end{equation}.
